iti110:1.1: This was said by the Blessed One, said by the Arahant, so I have heard:
iti110:2.1:
“If, while he is walking, monks, there arises in a monk a thought of sensuality,
a thought of ill-will, or a thought of harmfulness, and he does not quickly abandon, dispel,
iti110:2.2: demolish, or wipe that thought out of existence,
iti110:2.3:
then a monk walking with such a lack of ardency & compunction is
called continually & continuously lethargic & low in his persistence.
“If, while he is standing.…
“If, while he is sitting.…
iti110:5.1:
“If, while he is lying down, there arises in a monk a thought of sensuality, a thought of ill-will,
or a thought of harmfulness, and he does not quickly abandon, dispel, demolish,
iti110:5.2: or wipe that thought out of existence,
iti110:5.3:
then a monk lying down with such a lack of ardency & compunction is
called continually & continuously lethargic & low in his persistence.
iti110:6.1:
“But if, while he is walking, there arises in a monk a thought of sensuality,
a thought of ill-will, or a thought of harmfulness, and he quickly abandons, dispels, demolishes,
iti110:6.2: & wipes that thought out of existence,
iti110:6.3:
then a monk walking with such ardency & compunction is called continually & continuously resolute,
one with persistence aroused.
“If, while he is standing.…
“If, while he is sitting.…
iti110:9.1:
“If, while he is lying down, there arises in a monk a thought of sensuality,
a thought of ill-will, or a thought of harmfulness, and he quickly abandons, dispels, demolishes,
iti110:9.2: & wipes that thought out of existence,
iti110:9.3:
then a monk lying down with such ardency & compunction is called continually & continuously resolute,
one with persistence aroused.”
iti110:11.1: is following no path at all,
iti110:11.3: He’s incapable, a monk like this,
iti110:12.4: delighting in the stilling of thought:
iti110:12.5: he’s capable, a monk like this,
